data.title: VIG: The Stunning Dividend Powerhouse With 857% Dividend Growth Since Inception
data.description:
Sometimes, total return can be sacrificed because an investor seeks other targets. One could invest in dividend growth to create a steady income stream for retirement. And yet, if the total return is decent, one is even happier. Well, the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VIG) seems a perfect fit to achieve dividend growth with rather good total returns. In fact, since its inception in 2006, it has returned 461% vs. the S&P 500, which returned 524%.

In the meantime, it has handsomely compounded its dividend, which has increased by 857%. In a few years, we should see it up 10-fold since inception, which is quite an achievement for those who invested in it in their youth, believing that, by the time of retirement, they would have enjoyed a nice extra income.

Don't be mistaken, tough. The starting yield is just 1.84%, while SPY yields 1.27%. So, if you were to invest in VIG right now, you would have a yield below inflation and way below what Treasuries currently offer. At the same time, since its inception, VIG has compounded its dividend at a 10.5% annual rate, while SPY has achieved only 6%. So, it is not surprising that, especially during bear markets, VIG outperformed SPY and beat it also as total returns.

In fact, VIG's focus on picking the best dividend growers in the market makes it a more defensive holding than the market, since dividend payers usually have lower beta. This is also part of the reason why Morningstar awards the ETF a gold rating.

What are VIG's main characteristics?

The ETF tracks the S&P US Dividend Growers Index, whose main inclusion criterion is U.S. stocks that have increased their dividend for at least a decade. The index is market-cap weighted, and it also has two interesting rules: the highest-yielding names are not considered because the expectation that they will keep paying their dividend is lower; no stock can exceed 4% of the portfolio. Overall, there are more than 300 stocks in the index. If a company misses a dividend, then it will be excluded from the index, and it will have to wait another decade of dividend increases before being included again.

Its top 10 holdings right now are mainly from big tech and financials, which currently make up 23.1% and 22.55% of the stocks the ETF holds.

In the technology sector, we find big tech: Apple has a 3.89% weight, followed closely by Microsoft with a 3.87% weight. In third place, we have Broadcom and right behind it, JPMorgan Chase (3.61% and 3.58% weight, respectively). Visa and Mastercard are also included, as well as Exxon and even Costco. Overall, the top 10 holdings make up 31% of the portfolio, so this is a rather top-heavy ETF.

Other notable stocks are included. For example, The Procter & Gamble Company, which is the 11th holding with a 2.08% weight. Johnson & Johnson, Walmart, AbbVie, The Home Depot, Bank of America, The Coca-Cola Company also hold top spots right behind P&G. We also find companies such as Oracle (1.17% weight), McDonald's (1.16% weight), PepsiCo (1.07% weight) Accenture (1.01% weight), S&P Global (0.84% weight), Caterpillar (0.83% weight) and Union Pacific (0.74%). These are all popular stocks among dividend growth investors. The ETF also included Starbucks with a 0.54% weight. I have recently argued that I see 3 red flags that endanger its dividend. But the weight of this stock on the ETF is very low, so it won't even be perceived if Starbucks happens to cut its dividend.

Since it is a Vanguard ETF, we expect very low expenses, and, in fact, the expense ratio is only 0.05%. Over time, its AUM has consistently increased and is now above $100B. The ETF is also highly liquid, with an average daily share volume close to 1.3M, with average daily dollar volume of $245M (the median of all ETFs is 50K average share volume and $1.84M average dollar volume).

Its turnover is also low, around 11% (vs. 30% as the median of all ETFs).

While we consider very broad ETFs, it is very hard to find ones that are superior to SPY when considering total returns, VIG offers quite an interesting option because it performs similarly, while providing much faster dividend growth. Moreover, the ETF is built on a thoughtful strategy that strengthens those constituents that historically offer quality and stability. To this extent, VIG is an easy pick to bolster the defensive characteristics of a portfolio, without sacrificing gains and dividend growth.

We should not forget that when interest rates are high, investors often allocate assets to attractive fixed-income alternatives such as high-yielding bonds. This creates a shift away from dividend growers. However, although the FED is quite cautious with rate cuts, we can assume that, down the road, we will see interest rates moderating. In this case, bonds will be less attractive, and we could slowly see a new rotation back to dividend growers. This could constitute a further tailwind for the ETF that would see above-average returns due to a higher influx of new funds.

This is why I consider the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares a good pick. Right now, the price is recovering from the Liberation Day drop when it bottomed at $173. However, since the price is $192, we are still below the ATH of $205. The average PE of the index is 20.17, which is below the market average. The LT earnings growth rate is around 10%, which means the implied PEG is 2. Usually, this is considered expensive relative to the growth rate. However, we should also consider that this ETF deserves a premium due to the criteria used to build it.

Through this ETF, we can gain exposure to a broad and carefully selected range of high-quality dividend growth stocks at a decent price that reflects both a premium and a reasonable multiple. Perhaps, a better way to build a position could be to dollar-cost average every quarter. But, in any case, I consider the ETF a buy.

data.title: Lemonade: The Future Of Insurance Is Still Trading At Early-Stage Levels
data.description:
To me, there has never been a better time to increase your exposure to small-cap stocks. Recent market volatility has caused smaller-cap, traditionally more macroeconomically sensitive stocks to underperform versus the titans of the S&P 500. That gap creates a great opening for long-term oriented investors to pick up shares of fantastic growth stocks at a discount.

One of the most unique plays that I favor is Lemonade (LMND), the tech-enabled insurance company that has recently expanded its home and renters' insurance portfolio to include car insurance (at a time that insurance fundamentals are very healthy in the auto industry, thanks to recent regulatory approvals for rate increases). Year to date, shares of Lemonade have lost ~15% of their value, creating a great opening for a new position.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Lemonade in February, prior to the tariffs-driven market crash and when Lemonade was still trading near $31 per share. Since then, Lemonade has continued to post accelerating premium growth, while loss ratios continue to trend downward. With Lemonade hitting all of the right fundamental notes and barely seeing any stock price appreciation, I'm reiterating my buy opinion on this name.

One single chart should serve to summarize the bull case for Lemonade, and that's shown below:

Over the past six quarters, Lemonade has shown accelerating trends in in-force premiums (the total recurring value of all of Lemonade's customer premiums, before the company cedes a portion to its reinsurers), while growing economies of scale have continued to push Lemonade's gross loss ratios downward (the company remarked that its most recent trailing twelve-month gross loss ratio of 73% was well within the company's tolerance levels, and that includes the one-time impact of LA wildfires).

A reduction in policy loss ratios is also joined by increasing economies of scale on the opex side, as Lemonade benefits in sales and marketing from greater scale as well as utilizing AI to speed up claims processing and removing manual labor. These moves are expected to help push Lemonade toward an adjusted EBITDA breakeven in FY26.

To me, Lemonade is rapidly becoming the gold standard for insurance of the future. It bypasses clunky marketplaces and agent-based sales processes to give quick quotes on a clean interface on its website, and its policy options now offer a wide spectrum of coverage ranging from home, renters, pet, car, and term life insurance (I have firsthand experience as a customer, with Lemonade as my home insurance provider).

I encourage investors to pick up this stock as it continues to gain momentum, while still being relatively unnoticed by the broader stock market.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Lemonade's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

As previously shown, Lemonade's in-force premium grew 27% y/y to $1.01 billion, accelerating one point versus 26% y/y growth in the preceding Q4. Meanwhile, revenue, which is Lemonade's cut of its premium stream after its reinsurance contracts, grew 27% y/y as well to $151.2 million, handily beating Wall Street's expectations of $145.0 million (+22% y/y) by a five-point margin.

One major driver of growth for Lemonade has been its recent push into car insurance. Management views a broad cross-selling opportunity available for Lemonade Car, as the company currently has over 2.5 million customers that can be offered a bundling opportunity. Cross-sales into existing Lemonade customers increased 100% y/y in Q1, and the company launched its car insurance in Colorado, the first of several planned new-state rollouts in 2025. Still today, Lemonade Car is only active in ~40% of the U.S. population, giving a wide breadth for continued expansion.

Lemonade's ability to continue growing premiums is a testament to the fact that its business is almost entirely macro-resistant. Even though consumers are pulling back on discretionary spending, Lemonade continues to see blazing growth trends. The only potential risk (that we have not yet seen materialize) is the possibility of continued weakness in home sales to slow down new home policy growth rates.

In keeping with the strength of its recent trends, Lemonade boosted its full-year revenue expectations to $661-$663 million (25-26% growth), versus a 24-25% range previously.

On the profitability front, we note that Lemonade's adjusted EBITDA declined to a -$47.0 million loss in the quarter. This was driven by a temporary spike in net loss ratios to 82%, up 4 points from 78% in the year-ago Q1:

The LA wildfires, in particular, contributed to a good chunk of this quarter's losses. In analyzing the impacts of the (hopefully) one-time event on Lemonade's results, the company noted that it suffered a $36 million gross loss (before reinsurance and other adjustments) and a $22 million underlying impact to adjusted EBITDA - without which the company's adjusted EBITDA losses would have improved on a year-over-year basis.

Risks and key takeaways

Amid this quarter's strength, we should proactively mention two risks that Lemonade is facing.

The first is that the company has noted a slight weakening in retention rates in its home policies. Per CFO Tim Bixby's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Annual retention, or ADR, was 84%, a 4% decrease since this time last year, and down slightly versus 86% in the prior quarter. In broad strokes, we saw an unfavorable impact to ADR from our continuing effort to improve the profitability of our home book of about 4 points, about 2 points unfavorable from our pay per-mile car product, and about 2 points favorable from the rest of the book. We expect ADR to normalize and resume improvement over the coming quarters."

As mentioned, this was largely management-driven as the company tightened its underwriting standards and rejected renewals of home policies that it deemed risky. The result has been the improvement in gross loss ratios that we're seeing, and so far new business and cross-sales into products like Lemonade Car have helped to blunt the impact of weaker renewals. If new policy growth slows, however, we could start to see a deeper impact from weaker renewals.

The second is the potential impact from tariffs, particularly the 25% levy on auto parts. The company notes that if these rates remain in place, Lemonade would see a "single digit percentage point" impact on gross loss ratios in the Auto book alone, impacting vehicle replacement costs.

That being said, I see far more positives than negatives for Lemonade as it scales toward its goal of breakeven adjusted EBITDA by FY27. Its accelerating IFP growth is a testament to the growing popularity of its easy-to-use insurance platform, particularly among the younger generations as they enter prime home and car-buying years. Stay long here.

data.title: Northern Trust: Well-Positioned To Withstand Stagflation (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Northern Trust (NASDAQ:NTRS) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 16%, though they are more than 10% below their highs. Shares have recovered all of their “Liberation Day” losses, which makes sense given a business model with limited tariff exposure. While shares have been a meaningful long-term underperformer, Northern Trust has outperformed since I last covered shares in October 2023 when I rated it a sell. After the pressures from the Silicon Valley Bank failure, we have seen net interest and deposit trends improve, while buoyant markets have lifted fee revenue. With significant macro uncertainties, now is a good time to revisit NTRS. I see shares around fair value.

In the company’s first quarter, reported back on April 22nd, Northern Trust earned $1.90, beating consensus by $0.06 as revenue jumped by 18% to $1.95 billion. I would note that last year, Northern Trust took $189 million of losses on its securities portfolio. That represents just over 10% of the revenue growth. Otherwise, growth would be mid-single digits, still an encouraging outcome. The business has benefited from extremely strong capital and strong pricing power over its institutional deposit base.

Northern Trust is a custody bank in an oligopoly dominated by it, State Street (STT), and Bank of New York Mellon (BK). Unlike most commercial banks, these banks have little retail deposit presence and balance sheets that generally hold cash and securities rather than having significant loan portfolios. Instead, they are key members of the “plumbing” of the financial system by maintaining custody (or holding) the assets of investment funds, institutions, etc.

This is a relatively low-fee and low-risk business. It now has $16.9 trillion of assets under custody/administration, up 3% from last year. That means its revenue was just about ~4bps on its assets. Given low fee rates, it is essential to have a significant scale, which provides a strong moat around the three players. Ultimately, Northern Trust’s growth in assets under custody will mostly be tied to market levels, as market share movements are minimal.

NTRS earns revenue in two ways, primarily: by collecting fees on the assets it oversees, and by earning interest spreads on its deposits. Asset servicing fees rose by 5% from last year to $672 million, which was 2% faster than asset growth. This business has faced ongoing fee pressure, and so the faster revenue growth is a very welcome development. We are seeing some momentum in more value-added services. Most notably, securities lending is up to 23% of revenue. Now, it only has $191 billion in assets lent out, which is only about 1.5% of its assets.

In other words, securities lending drives 15x as much revenue per dollar of assets as the total business. AUM was up 17% from last year, which drove 9% more revenue. Revenue growth lagged AUM growth, suggesting NTRS earned lower fees to grow the business. I still view this as a winning action because increasing growth at the highest-margin segment is accretive to the total business, even if its standalone pricing becomes a bit less attractive.

Additionally, NTRS has an investment management operation, highlighted by its strong wealth management presence. Wealth management AUM rose by 6% to $447 billion, while assets under custody rose by 2% to $1.1 trillion. Wealth management fees rose by 8% to $542 million. They were down 1% sequentially. Again, I was encouraged to see revenue rise more quickly than overall AUM. Revenue here will be tied to market levels, which is a reason why Q1 was a bit lower than Q4.

Alongside this fee revenue growth, noninterest expense was up 4% to $1.42 billion. This would have been up 5%, excluding the impact of the FDIC special assessment last year. This is a fairly elevated pace of spending growth, as NTRS invests in upgrading technology and enhancing offerings. Still, after this year, I would like to see expense growth slow to match some of the efficiency efforts at peers like BNY.

Because of its current level of spending, NTRS is not enjoying much bottom-line improvement in its core fee business, instead making everything on net interest income. In Q1, it generated $521 million of pre-tax income, meaning it lost $52 million outside of its net interest income. Last year, it similarly lost about $55 million, adjusting for its securities loss. BNY is now able to turn a modest profit outside of net interest income (NII), which is where I would like to see NTRS get. That is unlikely to occur before 2027, in my view.

Turning to net interest trends, I am very encouraged by its ability to reprice deposits without disrupting flows. Its net interest margin ((NIM)) rose by 8bps from last year to 1.69%, down just 2bps sequentially. Given Fed rate cuts lowered FFE by about 30bps on average, less than 7% of this flowed through to NTRS’s margin. As explained on the earnings call, Northern Trust has achieved an 86% deposit beta this cycle, vs. 72% during the tightening. It is bringing rates back down on deposits more aggressively than it raised them. Its ability to isolate out institutional deposits, where it has a near-100% beta, is leading to this ability to recapture margin.

Now, if we did see the Fed funds rate drop below 250–300bps, I expect deposit betas would slow a bit, leading to some NII pressure. However, in this environment, that seems unlikely to occur, especially with inflation risk rising amid tariffs. That should support ongoing growth in NII. In Q1, net interest income was up 7% to $574 million. This was due to NIM expansion and the fact that average earning assets rose by 3% to $138 billion.

At the same time, its portfolio is short-dated with over 25% in cash, which reduces mark-to-market risks if rates surge. Similarly, if we see an economic downturn, its portfolio has limited exposure. NTRS has just $41 billion in loans, down $600 million from last year. These are largely high-quality loans to institutional borrowers with limited credit risk. As such, it took just a $1 million provision for credit losses. NTRS has $207 million of credit reserves, meaning allowances are 2.3x nonaccrual loans. This is a strong level that should limit the need to add reserves, even in a downturn.

Northern Trust has $109 billion of interest-bearing deposits, up 11% from last year. We do continue to see a negative mix shift, with $22 billion of noninterest-bearing deposits, down 14% from last year. Just 15% of deposits are NIB, and I expect balances to be fairly flat from current levels.

Northern Trust has an excellent balance sheet. It has a 12.9% common equity tier 1 capital (CET1) ratio, up 50bps sequentially and 150bps from last year. As a result, it did $288 million of share buybacks, and its share count is down 5% from last year. It also pays a secure 3% dividend yield. NTRS targets an 11-12% capital ratio. With so much excess capital, it should have a ~100% payout ratio this year and likely in 2026 as well.

Alongside results, NTRS updated guidance. Expense growth should still be below 5% this year, which remains elevated in my view. Thanks to strong deposit growth and pricing power, management now sees middle single-digit NII growth, up from low single-digit previously, assuming stable NIB balances. Given my view that the Fed is unlikely to cut rates more than two times, I view this guidance as credible.

Given its lack of credit exposure and gearing to short-term interest rates, Northern Trust is relatively well-positioned for a stagflationary environment. With limited tariff exposure, I see about $8-$8.25 in earnings power. It should be able to return all of this capital to shareholders. I view an ~8% capital return yield as fair value, given its low margins and limited medium-term growth. As such, at $100, I view the stock as essentially at fair value. If we saw a pullback towards $90, I would buy shares. Similarly, if we saw it return to the highs, I would sell. But for now, NTRS is likely to be a market performer and a relative safe haven amid macro uncertainty.

data.title: Cisco, Walmart Set To Report Results As Markets Watch Out For Core CPI
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages pushed higher on Thursday after President Donald Trump announced a new trade deal with the United Kingdom. The agreement will provide increased access for billions of dollars of U.S. products, and eliminate numerous non-tariff barriers. "Markets only really care how far and how fast Trump retreats from tariffs", UBS' Paul Donovan said. The Fed, on Wednesday, kept its benchmark rate at 4.25%-4.50% for the third straight meeting, as widely expected, even as it sees the risks of inflation and unemployment rising. Equities saw a muted response to the Fed decision, but the S&P 500 managed to post a gain by the close thanks to a late rally following a report that the Trump administration is planning to rescind Biden-era AI chip curbs.

Investors will keep a close eye on economic events scheduled for next week, such as, NFIB Small Business Optimism Index, and Core CPI on Tuesday. Tuesday will also see the release of the UK's Unemployment Rate, and Japan's PPI. Meanwhile, Thursday will see the release of UK's first quarter GDP, and United States' Jobless Claims, and Core PPI.

Walmart (WMT), Intuitive Machines (LUNR), Cisco (CSCO), Boot Barn Holdings (BOOT), and Hertz Global (HTZ) will report earnings next week.

______________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, May 12: Fox Corp (FOXA), Acadia Healthcare (ACHC), Hertz Global (HTZ). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, May 13: JD.com (JD), Intuitive Machines. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, May 14: Cisco, Nextracker (NXT), Boot Barn Holdings. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, May 15: Walmart, Alibaba ADR (BABA). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: WW International (WW) and Wolfspeed (WOLF) have a higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include NewHold Investment Corp. III (NHIC), Asset Entities (ASST), and Regulus Therapeutics (RGLS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Compass Diversified (CODI), Myriad Genetics (MYGN), and Prothena (PRTA).

IPO watch: The lockup periods for Thumzup Media (TZUP), Gelteq (GELS), and Polyrizon are set to expire on Monday. While, Yuanbao (YB) is expected to price its IPO on Monday and begin trading on Tuesday. OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE) is also expected to begin trading on Tuesday.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Apple (AAPL), KKR & Co (KKR), Ford Motor (F), Visa (V), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Kenvue (KVUE), Target (TGT), Microsoft (MSFT), and Eli Lilly (LLY). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividends include Advanced Drainage (WMS) from $0.16 to $0.17, Everest Group (EG) from $2 to $2.10, Cutiss-Wright (CW) from $0.21 to $0.22, Chubb (CB) from $0.91 to $0.95, HNI (HNI) from $0.33 to $0.34, and Ingredion (INGR) from $0.80 to $0.82. Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: The three-day Consensus 2025 event will begin on Wednesday in Toronto. The crypto conference hosted by Coindesk is one of the most anticipated of the year. The Sohn Investment Conference will also begin on Wednesday in New York City. Meanwhile, the Oppenheimer Israeli Conference will begin on Friday and will see participation from Pagaya Technologies (PGY) and Perion Network (PERI).

Cisco Earnings Preview: Cisco is scheduled to announce its third quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting earnings per share of $0.92, on revenue of $14.05B, a 10% increase from last year. UBS expects Cisco to report revenue upside, driven by both core networking demand and Splunk. UBS, however, remains focused on potential headwinds from efficiency efforts at the US Federal government, but notes Cisco's exposure is manageable at only ~10% of rev. Regarding tariffs, the Bank says, levies should not materially impact the April quarter and most of the July quarter given the 90-day pause.

Walmart Earnings Preview: Walmart is set to announce its first quarter earnings next week. Analysts expect the retail giant to report a 3.3% decline in EPS to $0.58, on a revenue of $164.5B. Oppenheimer expects Walmart's EPS to fall below Street expectations, and expects management to likely reaffirm full-year guidance. The retailer recently noted a wider range of outcomes around its Q1 operating income, driven by less favorable category mix, additional expenses, and opportunities to invest in price.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
